findings.description: _render interpolates msg.to and msg.subject directly into CRLF-separated headers, allowing email/SMTP header injection (extra headers, BCC, body) via a subject or recipient containing carriage-return/newline characters.
sendmail is given a manually rendered header blob plus an unvalidated msg.to envelope recipient, compounding the header-injection exposure instead of using the safe MIMEText API.
Dedup key is the recipient address only, so two genuinely different messages to the same recipient are treated as duplicates and the second is silently dropped.

No try/except around the send loop: if one sendmail raises, flush propagates the exception, remaining messages are not attempted, and _pending.clear() on line 65 is skipped, leaving already-sent messages in the queue (broken partial-failure/retry semantics).
flush opens and closes a brand-new SMTP/TCP connection for every message instead of reusing a single connection, causing significant overhead under load.

_seen retains recipients permanently (only cleared via clear_dedup_cache), so after a flush a legitimate later message to the same recipient is blocked, and the set grows unbounded (memory leak in a long-running queue).
preview uses \n line endings and omits the From header, so it does not faithfully reflect the headers/format actually sent by _render.
enqueue performs no validation of msg.to (e.g. empty or malformed address), deferring all failures to send time.
field is imported from dataclasses but never used.
MIMEText is imported but never used (the safe email-construction path it enables was bypassed in favor of manual string rendering).
